mappings.rationaleAI: Cultural relativism, structural-functionalism, and interpretive approaches address definitions of culture (A.1), cultural comparisons (A.3), cultural adaptation (A.4), and cultural patterns (A.5). Ethnographic focus on small-scale societies addresses primitive cultures (B.1).
Four million years of prehistory addresses hunters and gatherers (B.1.a) and the origin of civilized societies (B.2).
Hominin evolution provides biological context for hunters and gatherers (B.1.a).
Archaeological reconstruction addresses primitive societies (B.1) and civilized societies (B.2).
Agricultural science provides context for horticultural societies (B.1.b) and settled agriculturists (B.1.d).
Industrialization directly addresses modern industrial civilization and mass society (B.3).
Social institutions provide definitions of culture (A.1) and address modern industrial civilization (B.3).
Personality psychology addresses culture and personality (A.2).
Challenging Western-centric perspectives addresses cultural comparisons and ethnocentrism (A.3) and cultural change (A.4).
Multiculturalism addresses cultural comparisons (A.3) and cultural change (A.4).
Skepticism toward grand narratives challenges definitions of culture (A.1) and addresses cultural change (A.4).
Worldwide integration addresses cultural change (A.4) and mass society (B.3).
Eighteenth-century intellectual movement addresses the origin of civilized societies (B.2).
Historical materialism addresses cultural change (A.4) and modern industrial civilization (B.3).
Identity formation through cultural belonging addresses culture and personality (A.2).
Ritual in group identity addresses cultural patterns (A.5) and cultural institutions (A.6).
Music across diversity addresses cultural comparisons (A.3) and cultural patterns (A.5).
Historiographical theory provides frameworks for the origin of civilized societies (B.2).
Systematic study of human behaviour provides definitions of culture (A.1).
Innovation addresses cultural change (A.4) and modern industrial civilization (B.3).
Cultural exchange networks address cultural adaptation and change (A.4).
Critique of civilization addresses definitions of culture (A.1) and origin of civilized societies (B.2).
Cross-cultural literary relations address cultural comparisons (A.3).
